tiddlers.title: BootScript
Code Tiddler
tiddlers.text:
// This code tiddler is run as soon as TWikki is loaded/reloaded
console.log('Hello from the Code Tiddler!');

tiddlers.title: Commands
tiddlers.text:
A **command** is a one-shot action you can run from the command palette. Press **Ctrl+K** (or **Cmd+K** on Mac) anywhere in TWikki to open it.

- Empty input, or a query beginning with `>`, shows the **command list** (filterable).
- Any other text searches your notes instead, reusing the same ranking the sidebar search uses.

Arrow keys move, **Enter** runs the highlighted command, **Escape** closes.

## Built-in commands

These ship with the `base` package, so they are always available.

### Notes

| Command         | What it does                                              | Event                          |
| --------------- | --------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| New note        | Create a new tiddler                                      | `tiddler.new`                  |
| Save            | Persist all changes to `localStorage`                     | `save`                         |
| Reload UI       | Re-evaluate templates and re-render — no full page reload | `ui.reload`                    |
| Open all notes  | Open every (non-system) note                              | `ui.open.all`                  |
| Close all notes | Close every open note                                     | `ui.close.all`                 |
| Toggle sidebar  | Show or hide the explorer sidebar                         | _no event — direct DOM toggle_ |

### Search & navigation

The palette **is** the navigator: type a note's name (no `>` prefix) to jump to it. No separate "Go to note" command is needed. See [[Help]] for the search-syntax modifiers (`tag:`, `pck:`, `type:`, leading `$`).

### Trash

| Command     | What it does                           | Event                            |
| ----------- | -------------------------------------- | -------------------------------- |
| Open trash  | Open the [[$TrashManager]]             | `tiddler.show` → `$TrashManager` |
| Empty trash | Discard every trashed note permanently | `tiddlers.trashed.empty`         |

### Settings

| Command       | What it does              | Event                               |
| ------------- | ------------------------- | ----------------------------------- |
| Open Settings | Open [[$Settings]] | `tiddler.show` → `$Settings` |

### Workspaces

| Command                  | What it does                                            | Event                     |
| ------------------------ | ------------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------- |
| Create workspace         | Prompt for a name; offer to clone the current workspace | `workspace.create.prompt` |
| Switch workspace: _name_ | One entry per stored workspace (provided dynamically)   | `workspace.load.prompt`   |

See [[Workspaces]] for the bigger picture and other ways to switch.

### Themes

| Command              | What it does                                         | Event          |
| -------------------- | ---------------------------------------------------- | -------------- |
| Switch theme: _name_ | One entry per installed theme (provided dynamically) | `theme.switch` |

See [[Themes]] for how the cascade composes.

### Packages

| Command                  | What it does                                                         | Event                |
| ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------- |
| Import package from URL… | Prompt for a JSON URL and load it as an [[$ExtensionPackages]] entry | `package.reload.url` |

### Sync (when configured)

The Gist sync setup in [[Synchronization]] adds three more entries — they appear in the palette only after sync is configured:

| Command                   | What it does                     | Event        |
| ------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ------------ |
| Synch: full (pull + push) | Full reconcile with the remote   | `synch.full` |
| Synch: push               | Send local changes to the remote | `synch.push` |
| Synch: pull               | Take remote changes locally      | `synch.pull` |

## Dynamic commands

The "Switch workspace: _name_" and "Switch theme: _name_" entries are **not** registered up-front. They come from **command providers** — functions the palette calls on every open, so the list always reflects the current state. This is how new workspaces or freshly imported themes show up without a reload.

```js
tw.extensions.registerCommandProvider('workspaces', () =>
  (tw.store.global.get('workspaces') || []).map(name => ({
    label: `Switch workspace: ${name}`,
    event: 'workspace.load.prompt',
    payload: name,
  })),
);
```

## Commands from extension packages

Optional packages add their own commands when loaded — for example a favourites plugin might add **Show favorites** and **Toggle favorite (active note)**. The set on **your** install is everything `tw.commands.all()` returns at runtime; the **<<plugins>>** macro on [[Plugins]] shows you which packages are currently loaded.

## Registering your own command

Add one entry:

```js
tw.extensions.registerCommand({
  label: 'My command',
  event: 'my.event', // or `run: () => { ... }` for an inline action
  payload: 'optional-arg', // forwarded to the event handler
});
```

Add many at once:

```js
tw.extensions.registerCommand([
  {label: 'Do thing A', event: 'thing.a'},
  {label: 'Do thing B', event: 'thing.b'},
]);
```

A `run` function is invoked directly. An `event` is sent through `tw.events`, with `payload` as the argument — so anything that subscribes to that event can be triggered from the palette. See [[Events]] for the bus, and [[Plugins]] for the lifecycle hooks where most plugins register their commands.

## Calling a command from a tiddler

Commands have no direct URL syntax of their own, but their `event` does. Any `msg:` link runs the underlying handler — the palette is just one front-end:

```
[Open Settings](#msg:tiddler.show:$Settings)
[New note](#msg:tiddler.new)
[Empty trash](#msg:tiddlers.trashed.empty)
```

See [[Events]] for the full `msg:` link syntax.

tiddlers.title: Concepts
tiddlers.text:
# Overview

- [[Macros]] - Reusable components which insert content or functionality into your notes.
- [[Plugins]] - Extented functionality which can customize the look and behaviour of the app.
- [[Themes]] - Stylesheets and Layouts to change the look of Twikki
- [[Tags]] - An easy way to organize your data
- [[Packages]] - A way to bundle tiddlers
- [[Commands]] - A way to run stuff from keyboard (Ctrl+K)
- [[Settings]] - Preferences for how TWikki looks and behaves, saved per-workspace or shared across all of them
- [[BootScript]] - A way to customize twikki early in boot process

- Shadow tiddlers (tagged with `$Shadow`) are included in source, expected to be there and we cannot start without them
- Packages are loaded from URLs when we start — [[$CorePackages]] first, then [[$ExtensionPackages]]:
  - Core packages: `base` — adds the basic useful functionality every TWikki needs
  - Extension packages: `icons`, `demo`, `themes` and `website` (which provides the content and functionality for the TWikki website)
  - Each package URL carries a flag: `force` overwrites local copies on load, `nooverwrite` leaves your edited copies untouched
- Tiddlers imported via a package are not automatically saved to your localStorage when you save
  - If you edit and save them then they will be
  - Once you save them, they will not be overwritten from the package until you delete them

tiddlers.title: ContentSections
tiddlers.text:
# Content Sections

A **section** lets a single tiddler hold several independently-addressable pieces of content. Any tiddler's text can expose sections, so you can pack — say — a stylesheet, a snippet of script and some notes into one tiddler and then link to, include, or read each part on its own.

> This page is about addressable **content** sections (the core `core.sections` feature). Don't confuse them with the collapsible [[Sections]] UI widgets (`<<Section>>` / `<<Expand>>` / `<<Expose>>`), which are a different thing entirely.

# Anatomy

A tiddler's text is an optional **preamble** (everything before the first `# ` heading) followed by zero or more sections. A section starts at a `# Name` heading and runs until the next `# ` heading (or the end of the text):

````markdown
Anything up here, before the first heading, is the preamble.

# Intro
type: markdown

The body of the **Intro** section.

# Styles
```css
.note { color: rebeccapurple; }
```
````

Each section behaves like a *mini-tiddler*:

* **Metadata** — optional leading `key: value` lines, parsed exactly like a tiddler source file. `tags:` is comma-split, and `true` / `false` are coerced to booleans.
* **Body** — everything after the metadata.

Only a level-1 `# ` heading (a single hash followed by a space) starts a section — `##`, `###` and deeper headings are ordinary headings *within* a section. A `#` line **inside a fenced code block is ignored**, so a `# comment` in your code never accidentally starts a new section.

# Section types

A section carries its own `type`, so it can render — or run — differently from its parent tiddler. The type is resolved like this:

1. If the body is **exactly one fenced block**, the fence is stripped and the info-string (the word after the opening ```` ``` ````) sets the type:

| Info-string | Resulting type |
|---|---|
| `js` or `javascript` | `script/js` |
| `css` | `css` |
| `json` | `json` |
| `html` | `html/template` |
| `md` or `markdown` | `markdown` |
| `x-twikki`, `keyval`, `list`, `table` | the same type |
| *(bare fence, no info-string)* | inherits the parent tiddler's type |
| *(any other word)* | used verbatim as the type |

2. Otherwise the type comes from an explicit `type:` metadata line.
3. Failing both, the section **inherits the parent tiddler's type**.

# Addressing a section

Reference a section with the `::` delimiter — `Title::Section` — anywhere a tiddler reference is accepted. (`:` is not a valid title character, so `::` is never ambiguous with the single-colon command syntax like `msg:search:...`.) Section names match **case-insensitively**.

* **Link** — `[[AuroraDark::StyleSheet]]` opens just that section as a read-only card.
* **Include** — `{{AuroraDark::StyleSheet}}` transcludes just that section into another tiddler.
* **In code & lists** — `tw.run.getTiddlerTextRaw('AuroraDark::StyleSheet')` returns only that section's text. If the `::` form doesn't resolve, it falls back to the whole tiddler, so it is always safe to use.

A section view is **read-only**: it has no delete, and its **Edit** button opens the *parent* tiddler instead (a section is never edited on its own). Section cards are also never added to the store — the parent tiddler stays the single saved entry.

# What sections are for

* **Packed themes** — a [[$Theme]] lists the stylesheets to apply, and a stylesheet can be a section. For example `[[AuroraDark::StyleSheet]]` pulls the CSS section out of the `AuroraDark` tiddler, letting one tiddler carry several typed sections. See [[Themes]].
* **Runnable code** — a `js` (or `javascript`) fenced section is typed `script/js` and is executed exactly like a code tiddler: on load, and again every time you save it. Add the `$CodeDisabled` tag to the tiddler *or* to the section to stop it running. See [[Concepts]].
* **Structured data** — `keyval`, `list`, `table` or `json` sections let one tiddler store several named, structured payloads.

# Notes & limits

* Duplicate section names: the **last one wins** (a warning is logged to the console).
* Only `# ` (level 1) starts a section; `##` and deeper headings stay inside the current section.
* Sections are part of the core platform — they work in **every** tiddler, independent of the plugin layer.

tiddlers.title: DataList
tiddlers.text:
A DataList tiddler is a tiddler of type `list`.

Such tiddlers are intended to store a list of data which could be a list of strings or more structured data.

Some basic examples are the [[$TiddlerTypes]] tiddler which stores a list of all the different tiddler types. By using the core function `getKeyValuesArray` we can parse such `key:value` lists into an array of objects with `{key, value}` pairs which is useful for storing simple configurations.

A more functional example would be [[$CorePackages]] which stores a list of URLs of packages to import. $CorePackages is interesting because it's just a bunch of strings `<url> flag,option` but the application parses each line to determine what flags and options apply to each url.

In future we will offer a new type `table` which clearly separates the fields and displays them as a table.

Note: For `list` tiddlers only those lines marked as list items (beginning with `* `) are considered for the list. Everything else is just like a comment for the reader.

tiddlers.title: Dump
tiddlers.text:
Dump your **entire** current workspace (everything under `/ws/<workspace>/` in local storage) to a
single file.
* <<dump.dumpButton>>: Download `<workspace>.workspace.json`

To restore, **drag a `*.workspace.json` file onto the window** (you'll see a drop overlay). This
will *delete and completely overwrite* your current workspace — you'll be asked to confirm first.

See also [[Backup]], [[Help]], [[Features]].

tiddlers.title: Events
ExamplePlugin
FAQ
tiddlers.text:
# FAQ

## Are `.css` files automatically applied?

**No.** A `.css` file (or any tiddler tagged [$StyleSheet](#msg:search:$tag:$StyleSheet)) is *not* applied just because it exists. The `$StyleSheet` tag is only a findability convention — it is never read when CSS is applied.

A stylesheet takes effect only when it is **listed in the active theme**:

* The [[$Theme]] tiddler names the active theme (e.g. its text is `[[$CoreTheme]]`).
* A theme tiddler is a bullet list of stylesheet links. The [[$CoreThemeManager]] reads that list, concatenates the text of each listed stylesheet, and injects it into the page via `adoptedStyleSheets`.
* Editing a stylesheet that belongs to the active theme updates the page live (no reload). Editing one that isn't listed has no effect until a theme references it.

See [[Themes]] and [[Tags]] for more.

tiddlers.title: Features
tiddlers.text:
- [[Packages]]
- [[Plugins]]
- [[Themes]]

## Simple Data Access

- Double-Click to edit
- Ctrl+Enter to save
- Deleted tiddlers can be restored via [[$TrashManager]]
- [[$Workspaces]]: Easily switch between different stores!
- Saving works out of the box (localStorage)
- Use the [[Backup]] button <<backup.backupButton>> to save a snapshot remotely
- Use the [[Synchronization]] button <<synch.full>> to synch to a remote

## Great Search

- [Search Tiddlers for the word 'welcome'](#msg:search:welcome) `msg:search:welcome`
- [Search Tiddlers with the tag:$Theme 'welcome'](#msg:search:"tag:$Theme") `msg:search:"tag:$Theme"`
- Hide whole groups of notes from results by tag (no renaming) with the `includeTags`/`excludeTags` settings — themes and stylesheets are hidden by default. See [[Help]] and [[Tags]].
- Or create links to:
  - List all tiddlers
    - [All Normal Tiddlers](#msg:ui.open.all) `#msg:ui.open.all`
    - [Open Tiddlers beginning with A](#msg:ui.open.all:title:^A) `msg:ui.open.all:title:^A`
    - [Open Tiddlers tagged 'Favorite'](#msg:ui.open.all:tag:Favorite) ⭐

## Rendering

- Use [CommonMark](https://commonmark.org/) to format your text [example](#Markdown%20Test)
  - You can override this with a plugin or a custom renderer
- Code is automatically highlighted using [Highlight-JS](https://highlightjs.org/) (e.g. [js](#Code%20Tiddler), [css](#$CoreThemeLayout) and [html](#HTML%20Example))
- Include tiddler content with `{{$TWikkiVersion}}` = "{{$TWikkiVersion}}"
  - For more advanced examples install the <<packages.import name:demo url:packages/demo.json filter:\* force:false>> package and check out the [[SayHelloExamples]]

## Structured Data

- Configurable lists or key-value pairs via `list` tiddlers
  - e.g. Tiddler types from [[$TiddlerTypes]] tiddler
- Configuration objects via `json` tiddlers
  - e.g. Backup settings for [[GistBackupPlugin]]

## Validation

- Markdown tiddlers are validated on save (e.g. to ensure macros run correctly)
- JSON tiddlers are validated on save
- Code tiddlers are validated (run) on save (unless tagged `$CodeDisabled`)

## Other/Tech

- Basic pub/sub events
- Vanilla JS, no complicated framework

tiddlers.title: Help
tiddlers.text:
# Help

A quick guide to getting things done in TWikki. See [[Welcome]], [[FAQ]] and [[Features]] for the bigger picture, and [[Concepts]] for how it all fits together.

## Opening & creating notes
* Click any link to open that note (tiddler) alongside the others.
* Press `Ctrl`/`Cmd`+`K` to open the **command palette**: type to search commands and notes, use the arrow keys to move, `Enter` to open, `Escape` to close. Prefix with `>` to list commands only.
* Create a new note from the command palette (*New note*) or the <<New>> button.

## Editing & saving
* **Double-click** a note to edit it.
* Press `Ctrl`+`Enter` to finish editing (save the note).
* Notes are validated when you save: markdown is checked, JSON must parse, and code tiddlers are run (see [[Features]]).
* Press the save <<Save>> button (or run *Save all*) to write your changes to the browser's `localStorage`.
* Notes tagged `$NoEdit` are read-only and cannot be edited; see [[Tags]] for all the special tags.

## Searching
* Type in the search box to find notes by title and text.
* To search hidden (system) tiddlers, type `$` followed by your query, e.g. `$theme`.
* Filter your search with fields, which can be combined with each other and with free text:
  * `tag:Styling` — only notes carrying that tag
  * `pck:website` — only notes from that package
  * `type:markdown` — only notes of that type
* Examples: [`msg:search:welcome`](#msg:search:welcome), [`msg:search:"tag:$Theme"`](#msg:search:"tag:$Theme").

### Hiding notes by tag
* Whole groups of notes can be kept out of the search results *and* the sidebar Notes list by tag, without renaming them. Two settings on the **Search** tab of [[$Settings]] control this:
  * `excludeTags` — notes carrying any of these tags are hidden. Default: `$NoSearch, $Theme, $StyleSheet`, so themes and stylesheets don't clutter your results.
  * `includeTags` — a whitelist. When set, *only* notes carrying one of these tags appear (leave empty to show everything). The whitelist is applied first, then the exclude list.
* These lists never trap a note: a `$`-prefixed search (e.g. `$aurora`) or an explicit `tag:` search (e.g. `tag:$Theme`) ignores both lists and always finds the match.

## Linking & including
* `[[Note Name]]` creates a link to another note.
* `#Note Name` is a hash link that opens a note; `#msg:event:param` fires an action (e.g. `#msg:search:welcome`).
* `{{Note Name}}` includes (transcludes) another note's content inline. Pass parameters with `{{SayHello|"John Smith"}}` — see [[Parameters]].
* `<<macroName>>` runs a macro and inserts its output.

## Backup & sync
* Your data is saved locally in `localStorage` out of the box.
* Use the backup <<backup.backupButton>> button to save a snapshot to a remote (a private GitHub Gist).
* Use the synch <<synch.full>> button to keep a remote in sync with your local store.
* Notes tagged `$NoBackup` are never sent to the remote and are kept across a restore — useful for secrets and machine-local settings. See [[Tags]].

## Deleting & restoring
* Delete a note from its edit form. Deleted notes go to the trash, not away for good.
* Restore deleted notes from the [[$TrashManager]].

## Themes
* Switch theme with the selector: <<ThemeSelector>>
* See [[Themes]] to learn how themes and stylesheets work, and [[Tags]] for the `$StyleSheet`/`$Theme` tags.

## Learn more
* [[Concepts]] — types of tiddlers and how behaviour is decided
* [[Tags]] — every special `$` system tag
* [[Workspaces]] — running isolated stores side-by-side
* [[Plugins]] and [[Packages]] — extending TWikki
* [[Features]] — a tour of everything TWikki can do

tiddlers.title: MacroExamples
tiddlers.text: <<macroExamples>>

tiddlers.title: Macros
tiddlers.text:
Macros are short, parameterised functions you call from any tiddler with `<<name args>>`. Register one from a `$Plugin`'s `init()` or a `$Script` tiddler:

```js
tw.extensions.registerMacro('greet', 'hello', name => `Hello ${name}!`, {
  description: 'Greet someone by name.',
  example: '<<greet.hello Marc>>',
});
```

The 4th argument is a `meta` object: `{description, example?, version?}`. The table below is built from that meta — describe every macro you register and it shows up here for free.

The `core` namespace gets short names automatically: `<<list>>` ≡ `<<core.list>>` (see [[StandardMacros]] for live-rendered examples and [[Parameters]] for the parameter syntax).

See also [[MacroExamples]]

<<macros>>

tiddlers.title: Markdown
Modules
tiddlers.text:
## Installed Modules

<<modules>>

Modules are part of the platform but are loaded via URL and stored in local storage. They are like the "OS" and are common to all workspaces. [Modules Docs](https://github.com/cawoodm/twikki/blob/main/docs/MODULES.md)

Each module is versioned independently and declares the platform release it was built for; the **Built for** and **Status** columns below show whether each one is compatible with the platform you are running.


### Updates
Modules are loaded once and cached in localStorage. You can `?update` or `?reload` them via the URL.

## Versioning & compatibility

Every core module carries two **independent** version numbers:

* **Version** — the module's own version, following [semver](https://semver.org): the **major** changes on a breaking API change (a method renamed or removed), the **minor** on a new feature, and the **patch** on a bugfix.
* **Built for** — the platform release the module was written against.

A module is compatible when the running platform shares its **major** version **and** is at least as new as the "built for" release — the same rule as npm's `^` (caret). So a module built for `0.23.0` runs happily on `0.23.x`, `0.24.0`, `0.99.0`, … but **not** on an older `0.22.x` (the platform is missing features the module expects) nor on a breaking `1.0.0` (a new major may have removed APIs the module relies on). In short: newer platforms stay backwards-compatible with older modules until the major version is bumped.

The **Status** column reflects this check, and there are two degrees of incompatibility:

* **✓ OK** — compatible with the running platform.
* **⚠ …** — a *minor* mismatch: the **same major** version but built for a newer release (or no platform field declared). This is only a warning — you can choose to install and run it anyway.
* **✗ …** — a *major* mismatch: a different major version, where a breaking change may have removed APIs the module relies on. This **cannot** be overridden.
* **n/a** — not a code module (the `core.defaults` shadow-tiddler list), so it carries no version and is exempt.

Compatibility is checked at startup, *before* any module runs and *before* anything is written to local storage. If a freshly-downloaded module is incompatible the platform halts the boot and opens a dialog, where you can:

* **Update** the modules (install the downloaded versions) — allowed for ⚠ minor mismatches, blocked for ✗ major ones;
* **Keep current versions** — discard the download and carry on with the modules you already have installed; or
* point at a different module source and re-check before deciding.

Because nothing is written until you choose **Update**, declining always leaves your installed modules intact. If you can see this page, every module listed above either is compatible or was a warning you chose to accept.

tiddlers.title: Offline
tiddlers.text:
## Offline & Install

TWikki is **offline-first**. After one online visit it keeps working with no internet — your notes are local, and the app itself is cached on your device. You can also **install** it as an app so it gets its own window and launcher icon.

### Install

When you open TWikki in a supported browser you'll see an *Install* / *Add to home screen* prompt (the address-bar install icon on desktop, the browser menu on mobile). Installing gives TWikki its own window, icon and launcher entry, and lets it launch straight into offline mode. You don't have to install to work offline — a normal browser tab works too — but installing makes it feel like a native app.

## How offline works

Two independent layers cover everything TWikki needs to boot and run with no network:

- **Your data** — notes never touch a server. They live in your browser's `localStorage`, or in IndexedDB if you enable the `IndexedDBStoragePlugin`. See [[Workspaces]] and [[Saves]].
- **The app shell + code** — a [service worker](https://developer.mozilla.org/docs/Web/API/Service_Worker_API) precaches `index.html`, the platform, the core [[Modules]] and the compiled [[Packages]] on first visit. On every later load the browser serves them from that cache, so a reload with no network boots the full app — not an error page.

Because both layers are local, an offline TWikki can create, edit, search, save and navigate exactly like an online one.

### First visit still needs a connection

The very first load fetches the shell, modules and packages over the network so they can be cached. After that one online visit you're good to go offline. (A truly air-gapped first run — `file://`, no server ever — isn't supported yet.)

### Requirements

- A **secure context** — `https://` or `localhost`. The published site (github.io) qualifies; note this if you self-host.
- The service worker only runs in the **built/deployed** app, not the `npm run dev` server — dev intentionally skips caching so you always see fresh sources.

### Self-hosting & Docker

TWikki always runs under the `/twikki/` base path (the published site lives at `/twikki/`, and the Docker image serves there too, redirecting from the root). The Docker image bakes the service worker in at build time, so a self-hosted container is offline-capable out of the box — no extra configuration. Remember the secure-context rule above: serve it over `https://` (or reach it via `localhost`) for the service worker to register.

## How updates work

There are two caches and they refresh in different ways — this matters when you want the newest version.

### App shell (service worker)

When a new build is deployed, the service worker downloads it **in the background** and takes over automatically on your next visit — no action needed. You always end up on the latest shell without a manual refresh.

### Modules

[[Modules]] are fetched once and cached separately in `localStorage` (they're the shared "OS" across all your workspaces). They are **not** re-downloaded on every boot. To force-refresh them, add a query parameter to the URL:

- `?reload` — re-download the modules.
- `?update` — re-download and re-check compatibility.

Each module is versioned and declares the platform release it was built for; on boot TWikki checks compatibility before anything runs and, if a freshly downloaded module doesn't match, halts and asks you to **Update** or **Keep current versions**. Full details — including the semver/​compatibility rules — are on the [[Modules]] page.

## Offline-tolerant package loading

Some [[Packages]] are set to reload on every boot. If you're **offline** and a forced package fetch fails, TWikki keeps the copy it cached on a previous online boot and shows a quiet info notice instead of a scary error — so a flaky or absent connection never breaks your wiki. A genuine fetch failure while **online** (or an offline boot with nothing cached) still surfaces the error as before.

## What still needs a connection

A few features talk to remote services and only work online:

- **Synchronization & backup** — Gist sync and backup push/pull to GitHub. See [[Synchronization]].
- **Package & theme import** from a URL.
- **Syntax highlighting** currently loads highlight.js from a CDN, so highlighted code blocks may be unstyled offline (the rest of the page renders fine).

These degrade rather than corrupt anything — when you're back online they work normally again.

tiddlers.title: Packages
tiddlers.text:
## Packages

A package is just a list of tiddlers in JSON format `{"tiddlers": [...]}` which is usually loaded via URL.

- Packages listed in [[$CorePackages]] are loaded when TW starts
- Packages listed in [[$ExtensionPackages]] are loaded when TW starts (unless `?safemode` is used)
- Any package can be imported via the `<<import>>` widget:
  - <<packages.import name:website url:packages/website.json filter:\* force:false>>
- To import a demo package click here:
  - <<packages.import name:demo url:packages/demo.json filter:\* force:false>>
  - You can view what is in that package by clicking this button:
    - <<button "Demo Package" search $pck:demo>>

tiddlers.title: Parameters
tiddlers.text:
Parameters are used to pass messages to objects or widgets.

For example `<<example.greet Mr. Smith>>` passes 2 string parameters (`"Mr."` and `"Smith"`) to the `greet` macro in [[ExamplePlugin]].

Since TWikki is text based the string `"red green blue"` must be parsed to produce parameters. We support 2 basic strategies for function parameters (named and unnamed).

## Quick Reference

| Description               | Macro                           | Command                          | Invocation                    |
| ------------------------- | ------------------------------- | -------------------------------- | ----------------------------- |
| Single string             | `<<m hello>>`                   | `#msg:m:hello`                   | `m("hello")`                  |
| String with spaces        | `<<m "John Smith">>`            | `#msg:m:John Smith`              | `m("John Smith")`             |
| Multiple strings          | `<<m foo bar>>`                 | `#msg:m:["foo", "bar"]`          | `m("foo", "bar")`             |
| Typed (number, boolean)   | `<<m 1 true>>`                  | `#msg:m:[1, true]`               | `m(1, true)`                  |
| Object (named parameters) | `<<m name:"Jo Do" age:22>>`     | `#msg:m:name:"Jo Do" age:22`     | `m({name: "Jo Do", age: 22})` |
| Object (JSON)             | `<<m {"name":"Jo", "age":22}>>` | `#msg:m:{"name":"Jo", "age":22}` | `m({name: "Jo", age: 22})`    |
| Array, spread (JSON)      | `<<m ["a b", 2]>>`              | `#msg:m:["a b", 2]`              | `m("a b", 2)`                 |
| Array as one argument     | `<<m [[1,2,3]]>>`               | `#msg:m:[[1,2,3]]`               | `m([1, 2, 3])`                |

Notes:

- Macros tokenize and type-coerce bare values (`<<m foo bar>>` → two args, `<<m 1 true>>` → number+boolean); commands pass bare values as **one raw string** (`#msg:m:1` → `m("1")`, `#msg:m:foo bar` → `m("foo bar")`) — use JSON for multiple or typed command arguments.
- In markdown **links**, spaces in a `#msg:` URL must be `%20`-encoded or the link won't render (see [Macros vs Commands](#Parameters) below).

## Unnamed Parameters

Tradional JavaScript functions accept the passing of multiple unnamed (or positional) parameters as in `function macro(p1, p2, p3)` which accepts 3 arguments. We can invoke it as such:

- `<<macro foo bar>>` becomes `macro("foo", "bar")` (unquoted space-separated strings)
- `<<macro 1 true car>>` becomes `macro(1, true, "car")` (literal numbers and booleans)
- `<<macro 1 "true car">>` becomes `macro(1, "true car")` (quoted strings with spaces)
- `<<macro 1 true ${foo()}>>` becomes `macro(1, true, foo())` (eval expression)

Note: Technically the parameters are parsed into an array which is then spread as it is passed to the function.

## Named Parameters

Modern JavaScript functions expect a single object as a parameter or what may be called named parameters:
Consider the `function greet({title, lastName})` which accepts 2 named parameters. It can be invoked as such:

- `<<greet title:Mr. lastName:Smith>>` yielding `greet("Mr.", "Smith")` (unquoted space-separated strings)
- `<<greet title:"Prof. Dr." lastName:"Bryson">>` yielding `greet("Prof. Dr.", "Bryson")` (quoted strings with spaces)
  The same rules for booleans `true/false` or numbers and eval expressions apply as above.

Notes:

- There may be no space before or after the `:` and properties may contain only characters `a-z`, `A-Z`, `0-9` or the `_` .
- Objects do not (yet) support dynamic/eval expressions like `foo:{fcn()}`

## JSON Parameters

Parameters starting with `{` or `[` are parsed as JSON (the same rule as [[Commands]]). Keys and strings must be double-quoted — this is strict JSON, not JavaScript:

- `<<greet {"name":"John Smith", "age":22}>>` becomes `greet({name: 'John Smith', age: 22})`
- `<<macro ["a b", 2, true]>>` becomes `macro('a b', 2, true)` (arrays spread as positional parameters)
- `<<fetch {"url":"https://example.com"}>>` — JSON is the way to pass values containing `:` or `,`

Invalid JSON (e.g. unquoted keys like `{name:"John"}`) falls through to the legacy positional tokenizer and will arrive mangled — when in doubt, quote every key.

## Arrays

A JSON array spreads as positional parameters, so to pass an actual array as a single argument, wrap it: `<<add [[1,2,3]]>>` yielding `add([1, 2, 3])`.
Legacy alternative: pass a csv string which the function then manually `split(',')`s — `<<selector red,green,blue>>` passes one parameter `"red,green,blue"` (because there are no spaces) to the [[SelectorWidget]].

## Command Parameters

TWikki supports [[Commands]] — events fired from links (`#msg:event:params`) or from elements with `data-msg`/`data-params` attributes. The parameter string is decoded (an optional `---enc:BASE64` prefix) and `$currentTiddler` is replaced by the title of the surrounding tiddler, then deserialized by its first characters:

- **Raw string** (the default) — anything not matching the rules below is passed through as one string, so titles with spaces are safe:
  - `#msg:tiddler.show:My Note` becomes `show('My Note')`
  - `data-params="$currentTiddler"` becomes `close('My Note')` (on a button inside the tiddler 'My Note')
- **Named parameters** — `key:value` pairs (as in macros, see above) become one object:
  - `#msg:search.advanced:pck:icons title:add` becomes `searchAdvanced({pck: 'icons', title: 'add'})`
- **JSON** — strings starting with `{`, `[` or `"` are parsed; arrays are spread as positional arguments:
  - `#msg:ui.open.all:{"tag":"Favorite"}` becomes `openAll({tag: 'Favorite'})`
  - `data-params='["a", "b"]'` becomes `handler('a', 'b')`
  - `data-params='"tag:x"'` becomes `handler('tag:x')` (quote a literal string that would otherwise parse as named parameters)
- **JavaScript** — strings starting with `${` are evaluated: `data-params="${1 + 2}"` becomes `handler(3)`

Notes:

- Since `:` is not a valid character in tiddler titles, a bare title can never be mistaken for named parameters.
- Bare values are **not** type-coerced (`#msg:foo:1` passes the string `'1'`) — use JSON or named parameters for typed values.

## Macros vs Commands

The two paths differ — what works in `<<a.macro ...>>` may not work in `#msg:...` and vice versa:

- **Bare strings**: macros tokenize and type-coerce them (`<<m 1>>` → number `1`, `<<m a b>>` → two args); commands pass them raw (`#msg:m:1` → string `'1'`, `#msg:m:My Note` → one string). For multiple or typed command arguments use JSON: `#msg:m:[1, "a b"]`.
- **Eval**: macros eval single `{expr}` tokens; commands eval whole `${expr}` payloads.
- **Only commands** support `---enc:` encoded payloads and `$currentTiddler`.
- **Invalid JSON**: macros silently fall back to the tokenizer (mangled args); commands warn in the console and pass the raw string.
- **Parse errors**: a named-parameter payload whose parsing throws (e.g. a failing `{expr}` value like `a:{boom}`) renders an inline error span in macros, but commands silently pass the raw string to the handler.
- **Event names must be `lowercase.dotted`** — an uppercase letter silently truncates the event name (`#msg:t.Xy:1` fires `t.`!). Macro names may contain uppercase (`<<core.AllTagsLinked>>`).
- **Spaces in `#msg:` links must be `%20`-encoded** — a raw space ends the markdown URL and the link won't render: `[OK](#msg:search.advanced:pck:icons%20title:add)`. The `data-params` attribute and `tw.run.sendCommand(...)` have no such limit; for awkward payloads prefer a `tw.ui.button` (its payload travels `---enc:`-encoded).

See [Parameters Docs](https://github.com/cawoodm/twikki/blob/main/docs/PARAMETERS.md)

tiddlers.title: Plugins
Saves
tiddlers.text:
Twikki automatically saves everything you do to the browser (localStorage).

# Cloud Saves

In order to secure your data or share it between devices you can push/pull or synch your data to the cloud:

- Synch: The [[$GistSynchPlugin]] saves all your data to a [GitHub Gist](https://gist.github.com/) which is free storage up to 100MB.
  - With a synch across multiple devices, the last update wins.
  - For more info see [[Synchronization]].
- Backup: The [[$GistBackupPlugin]] works in a similar fashion but it has no synch: you backup and restore everything regardless of what was changed when.
  - For more info see [[Backup]]

To get started, create a [Personal Access Token](https://github.com/settings/tokens) and copy it into your Settings -> Backup/Synch -> Gist. It is fine to use the same PAT for both but you should use a different gistId for safety.

# Full Dump

You can also [[Dump]] your data to a local '.workspace.json' file as a backup or share it with others.

This does not respect any of the backup/synch rules (i.e. not synching certain notes) - it is a mirror of your workspace's local storage.

tiddlers.title: Sections
tiddlers.text:
## Expandable Sections

<<Section name:"Click here" content:"For more content here...">>
<<Section name:WebsitePackage message:tiddlers.list payload:"$pck=website">>

## Expose: Render on Expand

<<Expose name:Theme message:tiddler.content payload:Welcome>>

## Inline Expandable Section

TODO: Style these as clickable/expandable

- <<Expand name:Theme message:tiddler.text payload:$Theme>>
- <<Expand name:Theme message:tiddler.text payload:$Theme>>

tiddlers.title: Settings
tiddlers.text:
$tags: Help

**Settings** are your preferences for how TWikki looks and behaves — things like whether changes save automatically, how open notes are arranged, and where your backups go.

Open them with the **⚙ Settings** button in the sidebar (<<Settings>>), or press **Ctrl+K** and type _Settings_. Each setting shows a short description and a control to change it; changes take effect immediately — there is no separate save step.

## Where a setting is saved

Every setting has a small **user** switch beside it that decides where the value is kept:

- **Off (the default)** — the setting applies to **this [[workspace|Workspaces]] only**. Good for things you want different per workspace, like the layout or your search filters.
- **On** — the setting is shared across **all** your workspaces. Good for things you want everywhere, like your backup account.

Flip the switch any time and TWikki moves the value across for you. If a setting isn't set in either place, TWikki falls back to a sensible **default**.

## Defaults come with the app

You never face a blank settings screen. TWikki — and every [[plugin|Plugins]] you add — comes with sensible defaults, and new settings simply appear when you install a plugin that needs them. You only ever change the few you care about.

## Secrets stay on your device

Passwords and access tokens — for example a GitHub token used by backup or [[sync|Synchronization]] — are **not** stored with your ordinary settings. They live in a separate **secrets** list that is **never synced or backed up** and never leaves this device.

A setting that needs a secret just points at it by name, written like `${secret:myToken}`. To manage your secrets, press **Ctrl+K** and choose **Edit secrets**, then add one line per secret:

```
myToken: ghp_xxxxxxxxxxxx
```

Because settings only _refer_ to a secret rather than containing it, you can sync or share your settings without ever exposing a password.

## See also

- [[Workspaces]] — what is kept per workspace versus shared across all of them
- [[Synchronization]] — backing up and syncing your notes

tiddlers.title: StandardMacros
tiddlers.text:
TWikki ships a set of standard macros (widgets) in the `base` package. Call them anywhere in tiddler content as `<<name params>>` — short names resolve to the `core.` namespace automatically (`<<list>>` ≡ `<<core.list>>`); `button` and `packages.*` live in their own namespaces. See [[Parameters]] for the parameter syntax, and [[Macros]] for the complete auto-generated reference of every registered macro.

## Buttons

| Widget | Description | Example | Live |
|---|---|---|---|
| `button` | Generic button: label, event to send, optional payload and id | `<<button "Open All Help" ui.open.all tag:Help>>` | <<button "Open All Help" ui.open.all tag:Help>> |
| `command` | Debug button labelled with the `msg:payload` it sends | `<<command ui.reload>>` | <<command ui.reload>> |
| `Reload` | Reload the UI | `<<Reload>>` | <<Reload>> |
| `Save` | Save all tiddlers (icon button) | `<<Save>>` | <<Save>> |
| `Settings` | Open the [[$Settings]] tiddler; optional icon size in px | `<<Settings 16>>` | <<Settings 16>> |
| `New` | Create a new tiddler | `<<New>>` | <<New>> |
| `ShowAllTiddlersButton` | Open all tiddlers matching `tag`/`title` (regex, `!` negates, `*` = all) | `<<ShowAllTiddlersButton tag:Help>>` | <<ShowAllTiddlersButton tag:Help>> |
| `CloseAllTiddlersButton` | Close all open tiddlers matching `tag`/`title` | `<<CloseAllTiddlersButton>>` | <<CloseAllTiddlersButton>> |
| `WorkspaceCreate` | Prompt for and create/clone a workspace | `<<WorkspaceCreate>>` | <<WorkspaceCreate>> |
| `TrashCanIcon` | Trash-can button showing the trashed-tiddler count; opens [[$TrashManager]] | `<<TrashCanIcon>>` | <<TrashCanIcon>> |
| `TrashEmptyButton` | Permanently delete all trashed tiddlers (asks first) | `<<TrashEmptyButton>>` | <<TrashEmptyButton>> |

## Pickers & Inputs

| Widget | Description | Example | Live |
|---|---|---|---|
| `ThemeSelector` | Dropdown to switch the active theme instantly | `<<ThemeSelector>>` | <<ThemeSelector>> |
| `WorkspaceSelect` | Dropdown to switch or create workspaces | `<<WorkspaceSelect>>` | <<WorkspaceSelect>> |
| `selector` | HTML `<select>` from csv values or `key:value` pairs | `<<selector red,green,blue>>` | <<selector red,green,blue>> |
| `TagInput` | Text input with autocomplete over all existing tags | `<<TagInput id:my-tags>>` | <<TagInput id:my-tags>> |

## Lists & Information

| Widget | Description | Example | Live |
|---|---|---|---|
| `list` | Bulleted list of tiddler links filtered by `tag`, `title`, `pck`, `type` (system tiddlers hidden by default) | `<<list tag:Help>>` | *below ↓* |
| `text` | Raw text of a tiddler | `<<text $Theme>>` | *below ↓* |
| `Section` | Block expander (`<details>`); static `content` or the result of sending `message`/`payload` | `<<Section name:"More" content:"Details here">>` | <<Section name:"More" content:"Details here">> |
| `Expand` | Inline expander — like `Section` but flows with text | `<<Expand name:"More" content:"Details here">>` | <<Expand name:"More" content:"Details here">> |
| `Expose` | Expander that renders the result of `message` only when opened | `<<Expose name:"Theme" message:tiddler.text payload:$Theme>>` | <<Expose name:"Theme" message:tiddler.text payload:$Theme>> |
| `AllTiddlersSimple` | All tiddler titles on one line, custom separator | `<<AllTiddlersSimple " - ">>` | *below ↓* |
| `AllTagsSimple` | All tags on one line, custom separator | `<<AllTagsSimple>>` | <<AllTagsSimple>> |
| `AllTagsLinked` | All tags as links which open the tagged tiddlers | `<<AllTagsLinked>>` | *below ↓* |
| `AllTypesMacro` | All tiddler types in use, as links opening tiddlers of that type | `<<AllTypesMacro>>` | *below ↓* |
| `TrashCanStatus` | One-line count of trashed tiddlers | `<<TrashCanStatus>>` | <<TrashCanStatus>> |
| `TrashCanContents` | List of trashed tiddlers with preview/restore links | `<<TrashCanContents>>` | *below ↓* |
| `modules` | Markdown table of installed core modules with versions | `<<modules>>` | *below ↓* |

## Packages

| Widget | Description | Example | Live |
|---|---|---|---|
| `packages.import` | Button importing a package from a URL (`name`, `url`, optional `filter`, `overWrite`, `doNotSave`) | `<<packages.import name:website url:packages/website.json>>` | <<packages.import name:website url:packages/website.json>> |
| `packages.importBin` | As above for binary packages | `<<packages.importBin name:icons url:packages/icons.json>>` | <<packages.importBin name:icons url:packages/icons.json>> |

## Live Examples (block widgets)

These widgets produce multi-line output, so they render below rather than inside the table:

`<<text $Theme>>` — the current theme: <<text $Theme>>

<<Section {"name":"All Help tiddlers — list tag:Help", "message":"tiddlers.list", "payload":{"tag":"Help"}}>>

All tags linked — `<<AllTagsLinked>>`:

<<AllTagsLinked>>

All types — `<<AllTypesMacro>>`:

<<AllTypesMacro>>

All titles — `<<AllTiddlersSimple " - ">>`:

<<AllTiddlersSimple " - ">>

Trash contents — `<<TrashCanContents>>`:

<<TrashCanContents>>

Installed modules — `<<modules>>`:

<<modules>>

tiddlers.title: Synchronization
tiddlers.text:
Synchronization replicates local changes to a remote server.

The standard setup uses [Github Gists](https://gists.github.com/) so create a gist and [Personal Access Token](https://github.com/settings/tokens) add your `gistId` and `accessToken` to [[$Settings]]

- <<synch.test>>: Simulate a Synch
- <<synch.full>>: Run full synch (pull+push)
- <<synch.push>>: Push changes to remote without pulling anything in locally
- <<synch.pull>>: Pull changes from remote without pushing anything to remote
- <<synch.upload>>: Push changes to remote overwriting everything

The synch algorithm is:

- Latest change/deletion wins
  - This includes local deletions in your trash - they will be deleted remotely unless the remote update is newer than the deletion
- Tiddlers tagged `$NoSynch` or marked `DoNotSave` are not considered for synching

tiddlers.title: Tags
tiddlers.text:
Tags are words used to describe (or "tag") our content and you can freely choose these.  
A tiddler's tags are displayed at the bottom of the tiddler as "pills" you can click on to view other tiddlers with the same tag.  
For example this tiddler is tagged with <<Tag Help>>.

## Special System Tags

By convention, tags beginning with `$` are *special system tags*. Some are purely a convention to make tiddlers easier to find (e.g. <<Tag $StyleSheet>>); others change how the platform treats a tiddler. To list every tiddler carrying a given tag, search for `$tag:<TagName>` (system tiddlers are hidden unless your search starts with `$`).

You can also hide whole groups of tiddlers from normal search *by tag* — without renaming them — via the `includeTags`/`excludeTags` settings on the **Search** tab of [[$Settings]]. By default `$Theme` and `$StyleSheet` are excluded. An explicit `tag:` search or a `$`-prefixed search always ignores these lists. See [[Help]] for details.

### Tags that change behaviour

* `$Shadow`: Marks a built-in (shadow) tiddler that ships with the platform source. Shadow tiddlers are expected to always be present, are hidden from normal searches, and are not written to your `localStorage` until you edit and save them. 
* `$NoEdit`: Protects a tiddler against changes. The edit form is disabled, attempts to update it throw a "read-only" error, and deleting it prompts for extra confirmation.
* `$NoImport`: Prevents a tiddler from being overwritten by a package import (see [[Packages]]).
* `$NoBackup`: Excludes a tiddler from backup. `$NoBackup` tiddlers are never pushed into backups. Useful for secrets and machine-local settings.
* `$NoSynch`: Excludes tiddlers from synch. `$NoSynch` tiddlers are never pushed into synchs. Useful for secrets and machine-local settings.
* `$CodeDisabled`: Disables a code tiddler (`script/js`) so its body is *not* run on load. Editing such a tiddler warns you that it is inactive; remove the tag to re-activate it.
* `$NoSearch`: Will be hidden from normal search results and lists.
* `$StyleSheet`: A *convention only* — it marks a tiddler as a stylesheet so it is easy to find (`$tag:$StyleSheet`); the tag itself does **not** cause the CSS to be applied. A stylesheet is applied only when it is listed in the active theme (see [[$Theme]] / [[$CoreThemeManager]]), which concatenates the text of its listed stylesheets into the page. Editing a stylesheet that belongs to the active theme updates the page live (no reload); editing one that isn't listed has no effect until a theme references it. Any `.css` source file is auto-tagged `$StyleSheet` at compile time. Hidden from normal search by default (see the `excludeTags` setting above).
* `$Theme`: Marks a tiddler as a selectable theme. Theme tiddlers are a list of stylesheets that the [[$CoreThemeManager]] applies as CSS; the active theme is named in the [[$Theme]] tiddler. Hidden from normal search by default (`excludeTags`).
* `$Template`: Marks a tiddler as a template (an HTML fragment used to render other content rather than read on its own).

tiddlers.title: Themes
tiddlers.text:
# TWikki Themes

TWikki's look is driven entirely by stylesheets which you can change or replace.

The current theme is always stored in the special [[$Theme]] tiddler.

A basic theme is just one or more stylesheets in list format:

```
* [[$CoreThemeLayout]]
* [[$CoreThemeAppearance]]
* [[AuroraTheme::AuroraPalette]]
```

It should be tagged <<Tag $Theme>> so the theme selector picks it up and the user can select it.

See [Themes Docs](https://github.com/cawoodm/twikki/blob/main/docs/THEMES.md)

tiddlers.title: TW API
tiddlers.text:
## The `tw` namespace

Everything hangs off the global `tw` object:

* `tw.events` — pub/sub: `subscribe(event, handler, name)` and `send(event, payload)`
* `tw.tiddlers` — the in-memory store (`.all`, `.visible`, `.trashed`)
* `tw.run` — read and update tiddlers, e.g. `tw.run.getTiddler(title)`
* `tw.ui` — UI helpers such as `tw.ui.notify(...)` and `tw.ui.button(...)`
* `tw.macros` / `tw.extensions` — register macros, widgets and command palette commands (see below)
* `tw.commands` — the command palette registry (`registerCommand` / `registerCommandProvider` via `tw.extensions`)
* `tw.core.*` — core subsystems (`dom`, `markdown`, `search`, `tiddlers`, `render`, `store`, ...)
* `tw.store` — the workspace-scoped storage API (`get`/`set`/`delete`/`keys`, raw `exportRaw`/`importRaw`, `global` for unscoped keys) — what modules and plugins should use
* `tw.storage` — the platform's raw localStorage wrapper (platform-internal; use `tw.store`)

tiddlers.title: USP
tiddlers.text:
# Why TWikki?

TWikki is inspired by [TiddlyWiki](https://tiddlywiki.com/) — it keeps the ideas that made TiddlyWiki great (small composable notes called *tiddlers*, "code is data", a single living document you can extend from the inside) and rethinks the parts that made it awkward in a modern, cloud-connected, multi-device world.

This page explains what TWikki does better. It's split into a section for everyday users and a section for technical users.

---

## For everyday users

### Saving just works
TiddlyWiki's most infamous quirk is saving. Because an `.html` file can't write to your disk, classic TiddlyWiki re-generates the *entire* HTML file every time you save and hands it back to you as a download — so you end up repeatedly re-downloading the document you're editing, or wiring up a plugin/helper app to do it for you.

TWikki removes that pain entirely. **Every change is saved automatically** to your browser's storage as you type. There's nothing to download and nothing to remember.

### Your notes follow you between devices
TWikki is cloud-native by design. Your data lives wherever you choose and is synced to whichever browser you open it in, so you can pick up on your phone where you left off on your laptop. Backup and sync to a private GitHub Gist is built in — one button to snapshot, one button to sync.

### Works offline
Everything TWikki needs is cached locally, so it keeps working on a plane, a train, or a flaky connection. It updates itself automatically when you're back online.

### Looks the way you want — instantly
Themes in TWikki switch and update **live, with no reload**. Pick a theme from a dropdown and the page changes immediately. Several polished themes ship in the box (light and dark), and you can tweak or build your own.

### Find things fast
Search is first-class. You can search by word or by tag, and you can hide whole groups of notes from results by tag — without renaming anything — using simple include/exclude settings. A built-in Settings dialog lets you configure this without touching any code.

### Nothing is ever really lost
Deleted notes go to a trash that you can restore from, and you can keep multiple separate stores ("workspaces") and switch between them.

### Write in plain Markdown
Notes are written in standard [Markdown](https://commonmark.org/), so the formatting you already know just works — headings, lists, links, tables, and automatically syntax-highlighted code.

---

## For technical users

### Code is data — the platform *is* the IDE
As in TiddlyWiki, everything lives in the store, including code. A tiddler of type `script/js` is executed (`eval`) in the global context when TWikki loads and again whenever you save it, so changes take effect immediately. **There is no separate plugin format: a plugin is just a tiddler.** Installing one means adding a tiddler; uninstalling means deleting it. Tag it `$CodeDisabled` to keep it dormant.

### A clean, framework-free runtime
TWikki is vanilla JavaScript — no React, no build-time framework, no bundler magic in the runtime. Modules are plain IIFEs loaded over HTTP and evaluated at global scope, hanging their exports off a single global `tw` namespace (see [[TW API]]).

This makes the system easy to read, debug, and extend without learning a bespoke widget/filter language.

### Data-driven theming, no build step
A theme is just a tiddler listing the stylesheets to apply. The theme manager concatenates those stylesheets and injects them via `adoptedStyleSheets`, so editing a stylesheet in the active theme updates the page live. Themes are ordinary data you can ship, import, and version like anything else. See [THEMES.md](THEMES.md).

### Single-file plugins and themes via sections
A tiddler can be split into named **sections** addressed as `Parent::Section`. This means an entire plugin — or a theme plus its stylesheets — can live in a *single* multipart tiddler instead of being scattered across many. See the [CHANGELOG](https://github.com/cawoodm/twikki/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md) and the in-app `ExamplePlugin`.

### Packages over URLs
A package is simply a JSON list of tiddlers (`{"tiddlers": [...]}`), usually loaded from a URL. Core and extension packages are declared in the `$CorePackages` / `$ExtensionPackages` tiddlers and fetched on start; any package can be imported on demand with the `<<packages.import>>` widget. Distribution is just "host a JSON file somewhere."

### Cloud-native storage model
Rather than rewriting one giant HTML file, TWikki persists each change to `localStorage` and syncs to a remote you control (e.g. a private GitHub Gist). Tags like `$NoBackup` / `$NoImport` give per-tiddler control over what syncs and what survives an import — useful for secrets and machine-local settings.

### Validation on save
Tiddlers are validated when you save: Markdown is checked (so macros render), JSON must parse, and code tiddlers are run (unless `$CodeDisabled`), surfacing errors immediately instead of at some later mystery moment.

### Macros, widgets, and transclusion
Register a macro with `tw.extensions.registerMacro(ns, name, fn)` and call it anywhere as `<<ns.name args>>`. Transclude tiddler content with `{{Title}}`, including positional, default, and named parameters (`{{SayHello|name:"Marc"}}`). It's the composability of TiddlyWiki's transclusion without the custom filter syntax.

### Built for debugging
Query-string switches make the platform transparent when something breaks: `?trace` disables the try/catch wrapping so real stack traces surface, `?debug` enables debug logging, `?reload`/`?update` force a fresh download, `?safemode` skips extension packages, and `?clear` wipes local storage. See [CLAUDE.md](../CLAUDE.md) for the full list.

---

See the [CHANGELOG](https://github.com/cawoodm/twikki/blob/main/CHANGELOG.md) for what's new, and [MODULES.md](MODULES.md) / [THEMES.md](THEMES.md) / [COMPILER.md](COMPILER.md) for deeper technical docs.

tiddlers.title: WebsiteLoadScript
Welcome
tiddlers.text:
# Welcome

TWikki is, like [TiddlyWiki](https://tiddlywiki.com/), a cool way to manage semi-structured information.
It runs completely in your browser and you can access it from multiple devices.

Create your first note with <<New>> or press Ctrl+K to begin searching content or running commands.

> Tip: type `$` in the search box to see hidden content

## Motivation

TiddlyWiki is an excellent and mature tool but we decided to improve it in a few key areas:

- Natively support local and cloud [[Saves]]
- Use standardized [[Markdown]]
- Simplify things for non-technical users
- [read on...](#USP)

If you are new to TiddlyWiki checkout [[Concepts]].

Checkout these cool [[Features]]:

- [[Themes]]
- [[Packages]]
- [[Plugins]]
- [[Help]]

---

NOTE: This 'Welcome' tiddler is part of the [package](#Packages) [website](#msg:search:$pck:website).

tiddlers.title: Workspaces
tiddlers.text:
A **workspace** is an isolated tiddler store. Every workspace has its own notes, its own [[$Theme]], its own settings — they live side-by-side under separate `/ws/<name>/` keys in `localStorage` and never see each other. You can use one for personal notes, another for a project, another as a sandbox to try plugins or themes without polluting your main store.

The active workspace per browser tab is held in `sessionStorage`, so opening two tabs and switching one does **not** disturb the other. Each tab is independent.

## Switching workspaces

The current workspace's name shows in the workspace picker in the header. Below are every way to change it.

### The picker widget

<<WorkspaceSelect>>

- **Left-click** an entry → switch in this tab (the page reloads in the chosen workspace).
- **Middle-click** (or Ctrl/⌘-click, right-click → "Open in new tab") → open the chosen workspace in a **new tab**, leaving this one alone. That is how you get workspace A and workspace B running side-by-side.

You can drop `<<WorkspaceSelect>>` into any tiddler — it is the same widget the header uses.

### From the command palette

Press **Ctrl+K** to open the command palette, then type `Switch` — every workspace is listed as `Switch workspace: <name>`. The same palette also has **Create workspace**.

### From a URL

Append `?ws=<name>` to the TWikki URL to open a tab directly in that workspace. The query is consumed on arrival: the name is written into the tab's session anchor and the URL is replaced with the clean one (no `?ws=`). For example:

- [Open the default workspace in a new tab](?ws=default) — `?ws=default`

The same trick is what middle-click in the picker does under the hood. If the name does not exist, the URL is left visible with a console warning and the tab boots into the global default.

### From a `msg:` link inside a tiddler

Anywhere you can write markdown, you can embed a switch link. The event is `workspace.load`:

| Link | Raw syntax |
|---|---|
| [Switch to default](#msg:workspace.load:default) | `#msg:workspace.load:default` |
| [Open the workspace picker](#msg:workspace.load.prompt) | `#msg:workspace.load.prompt` |

See [[Events]] for the general link format. Spaces in workspace names must be percent-encoded (`My%20Notes`).

## Creating a workspace

Three doors to the same prompt:

- The **– new workspace –** entry at the top of the picker above.
- **Create workspace** in the Ctrl+K command palette.
- The button macro: <<WorkspaceCreate>>

You will be asked for a name and then offered to **clone** the current workspace (copy its tiddlers, visible list, and trashed list across). Decline to start with an empty store.

## What is shared and what is not

| | Scope |
|---|---|
| Tiddlers (notes, tags, drafts, trashed) | **Per workspace** |
| [[$Theme]] selection and `$StyleSheetUser` | **Per workspace** |
| Settings tiddlers (`$Settings`, `$SearchSettings`, …) | **Per workspace** |
| List of all known workspaces | Global |
| "Last switched-to" workspace (the default for fresh tabs) | Global |
| The active workspace **for this tab** | Per tab (sessionStorage) |
| Module cache (no need to re-download core code per workspace) | Global |

## Limitations

- Switching workspace in the current tab triggers a **full page reload** — necessary because shadow tiddlers are frozen at boot and need a fresh load to swap out.
- Opening the **same** workspace in two tabs is allowed but **not synchronised**. Edits in one tab become visible in the other only after the other tab reloads — last write wins, by tiddler timestamp.
- Middle-clicking a regular wikilink (`[[Other Note]]`) opens a new tab in the global default workspace, **not** the workspace of the source tab. To open another workspace in a new tab, use the picker — its links carry an explicit `?ws=` target.

## Behind the scenes

Workspaces are scoped purely by a key prefix in `localStorage`. The `core.store` module reads `tw.workspace` on every key access and prepends `/ws/<name>/`, so every read and write inside a workspace is automatically isolated. The `core.workspaces` module manages which name is active and persists the list of known workspaces. See [the source for `core.workspaces`](https://github.com/cawoodm/twikki/blob/main/src/modules/core.workspaces.js) for the full lifecycle.
